title: A Little Mistake
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis a yarn I heard of a new-chum ן¿½trapן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the edge of the Never-Never,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the dead men lie and the black men lie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the bushman lies for ever.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas the custom still with the local blacks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To cadge in the ן¿½altogetherן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They had less respect for our feelings then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And more respect for the weather.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The trooper said to the sergeantן¿½s wife:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sure, I wouldnן¿½t seem unpleasant;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But thereן¿½s women and childer about the place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And barrinן¿½ a ladyן¿½s present

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thereן¿½s ould King Billy wid niver a stitch
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For a month may the drought cremate him!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Bar the wan we put in his dirty head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Where his old Queen Mary bate him.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½God give her strength! and a peaceful reign
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Though she flies in a bit av a passion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If ony wan hints that her shtoyle anן¿½ luks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Are a trifle behind the fashion.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thereן¿½s two of the boys by the stable now
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Be the powers! Iן¿½ll teach the varmints
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To come wid nought but a shirt apiece,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And wid dirt for their nayther garmints.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Howld on, ye blaggards! How dare ye dare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To come widin sight av the houses?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll give ye a warninן¿½ all for wance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Anן¿½ a couple of ould pair of trousers.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They took the pants as a child a toy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The constableן¿½s words beguiling
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A smile of something beside their joy;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they took their departure smiling.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And that very day, when the sun was low,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two blackfellows came to the station;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They were filled with the courage of Queensland rum
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bursting with indignation.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The constable noticed, with growing ire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½d apparently dressed in a hurry;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And their language that day, I am sorry to say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mostly consisted of ן¿½plurry.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The constable heard, and he wished himself back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the land of the bogs and the ditches
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You plurry big tight-britches pן¿½liceman, what for
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You gibbit our missuses britches?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And this was a case, I am bound to confess,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where civilisation went under;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had one of the gins been less modest in dress
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d never have made such a blunder.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And here let the moral be duly made known,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hereafter signed and attested:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We should place more reliance on that which is shown
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And less upon what is suggested.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
